problems.title: Contains Duplicate
problems.summary: Return true if any value appears at least twice in nums.

approaches.name: Hash set
steps: Create an empty hash set.
For each num: if num is already in set, return true.
Add num to the set.
Return false after processing all elements.
approaches.analogy: Like a bouncer's guest list — the moment someone walks in twice, you know there's a duplicate.

problems.title: Valid Anagram
problems.summary: Return true if t is an anagram of s (same letters, same counts).

approaches.name: Frequency count
steps: If lengths differ, return false.
Count character frequencies in s with a hash map.
Decrement counts for each char in t; return false on missing or negative.
Return true if all counts are zero.
approaches.analogy: Like comparing two Scrabble racks — if every letter count matches, the words are anagrams.

problems.title: Two Sum
problems.summary: Return indices of two numbers in nums that add up to target.

approaches.name: Hash map complement
steps: Create a map from value to index.
For each num at index i, compute complement = target - num.
If complement exists in map, return [map[complement], i].
Otherwise store num -> i in the map.
approaches.analogy: Like finding the missing puzzle piece — for each piece, check if its partner is already on the table.

problems.title: Group Anagrams
problems.summary: Group strings that are anagrams of each other.

approaches.name: Sorted string key
steps: Create a hash map from key to list of strings.
For each string, sort its characters to form the key.
Append the string to map[key].
Return all values in the map.
approaches.analogy: Like sorting each word's letters into a canonical form — anagrams collapse to the same fingerprint.

approaches.name: Frequency count key
steps: Create a hash map from frequency signature to list of strings.
For each string, build a 26-count key (or tuple of counts).
Append the string to map[key].
Return all values in the map.
approaches.analogy: Like tallying ingredients in recipe cards — same letter counts mean the same dish (anagram group).

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
result = Solution().groupAnagrams(["eat", "tea", "tan", "ate", "nat", "bat"])
assert sorted([sorted(g) for g in result]) == sorted([sorted(g) for g in [["bat"], ["nat", "tan"], ["eat", "tea", "ate"]]])

problems.title: Top K Frequent Elements
problems.summary: Return the k most frequent elements in nums.

approaches.name: Min-heap of size k
steps: Count frequency of each element.
Maintain a min-heap of size k by frequency.
For each (freq, num): push; pop smallest if size exceeds k.
Return all numbers remaining in the heap.
approaches.analogy: Like keeping only the k tallest people in line — evict the shortest whenever someone taller shows up.

approaches.name: Bucket sort by frequency
steps: Count frequency of each element.
Create buckets indexed 1..n where bucket[i] holds nums with freq i.
Scan buckets from highest freq downward, collecting until k elements.
Return the collected k elements.
approaches.analogy: Like sorting mail into pigeonholes by frequency, then grab from the fullest slots first.

problems.title: Product of Array Except Self
problems.summary: Return an array where each element is the product of all other elements in nums.

approaches.name: Prefix + suffix products
steps: Initialize output array of length n.
Fill left-to-right with running prefix products (exclude self).
Multiply right-to-left with running suffix products.
Return the output array.
approaches.analogy: Like each person in a line multiplying everyone else — track left-side and right-side totals separately.

problems.title: Encode and Decode Strings
problems.summary: Design encode/decode for a list of strings into a single string and back.

approaches.name: Length-delimited encoding
steps: Encode: for each string, write length + '#' + string content.
Join encoded parts into one string.
Decode: read digits until '#', parse length, slice that many chars.
Repeat until the encoded string is fully consumed.
approaches.analogy: Like shipping boxes labeled #5#apple — the size tag tells you exactly where each item starts and ends.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
codec = Codec()
assert codec.decode(codec.encode(["hello", "world"])) == ["hello", "world"]

tests.label: Test 2
tests.script:
codec = Codec()
assert codec.decode(codec.encode([""])) == [""]

tests.label: Test 3
tests.script:
codec = Codec()
assert codec.decode(codec.encode(["a", "ab", "abc"])) == ["a", "ab", "abc"]

problems.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
problems.summary: Return the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence in nums.

approaches.name: Hash set streak expansion
steps: Insert all numbers into a hash set.
For each num, only start if num-1 is not in set (sequence start).
Expand num, num+1, num+2... while in set, tracking streak length.
Return the maximum streak length found.
approaches.analogy: Like spotting domino chains — only start counting from the first tile of each consecutive run.

problems.title: Valid Sudoku
problems.summary: Determine if a 9x9 Sudoku board is valid (rows, columns, boxes).

approaches.name: Hash sets per row/col/box
steps: Create hash sets for each row, column, and 3x3 box.
For each filled cell, compute box index from row/col.
If digit already seen in row, col, or box, return false.
Return true if no conflicts found.
approaches.analogy: Like three bouncers at a Sudoku party — each row, column, and box keeps its own 'already seen' list.

problems.title: Valid Palindrome
problems.summary: Return true if s is a palindrome after considering only alphanumeric chars.

approaches.name: Two pointers inward
steps: Set left = 0 and right = len(s) - 1.
Skip non-alphanumeric chars at both pointers.
Compare lowercase versions; return false on mismatch.
Return true when pointers cross.
approaches.analogy: Like reading a palindrome from both ends — skip junk, compare inward until they meet or mismatch.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.input: isPalindrome("A man, a plan, a canal: Panama")
tests.call: Solution().isPalindrome("A man, a plan, a canal: Panama")

problems.title: Two Sum II
problems.summary: In a sorted array, find two numbers that add up to target (1-indexed).

approaches.name: Sorted two pointers
steps: Set left = 0 and right = len(numbers) - 1.
Compute sum = numbers[left] + numbers[right].
If sum equals target, return [left+1, right+1].
Move left up if sum too small, else move right down.
approaches.analogy: Like two shoppers on a sorted price list — one at the cheap end, one at the expensive, meeting in the middle.

problems.title: 3Sum
problems.summary: Return all unique triplets in nums that sum to zero.

approaches.name: Sort + two pointers
steps: Sort nums ascending.
For each index i, run two pointers on i+1..end for -nums[i].
On match, record triplet and skip duplicate values at both pointers.
Return all collected triplets.
approaches.analogy: Like fixing one person in a sorted line and having two friends walk inward to find a matching pair for zero sum.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
result = Solution().threeSum([-1, 0, 1, 2, -1, -4])
assert sorted([sorted(t) for t in result]) == sorted([sorted(t) for t in [[-1, -1, 2], [-1, 0, 1]]])

problems.title: Container With Most Water
problems.summary: Find two lines that form a container holding the most water.

approaches.name: Greedy two pointers
steps: Set left = 0 and right = len(height) - 1.
Compute area = min(height[left], height[right]) * width.
Track maximum area seen.
Move the pointer at the shorter line inward.
approaches.analogy: Like two walls closing in — always move the shorter wall inward, since the taller one still has hope.

problems.title: Trapping Rain Water
problems.summary: Compute how much rain water can be trapped between elevation bars.

approaches.name: Two pointers with max heights
steps: Set left/right pointers and track leftMax and rightMax.
Process the side with smaller max height.
Add trapped water as maxHeight - height[pointer] if positive.
Move that pointer inward and update its max.
approaches.analogy: Like two people at opposite ends of a valley — each tracks the tallest wall they've seen to estimate trapped water.

problems.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
problems.summary: Return the maximum profit from one buy and one sell transaction.

approaches.name: Track min price + max profit
steps: Initialize minPrice to infinity and maxProfit to 0.
For each price, update minPrice to the lowest seen so far.
Update maxProfit with price - minPrice.
Return maxProfit after one pass.
approaches.analogy: Like buying at the lowest price seen so far and selling at today's price for the best profit.

problems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
problems.summary: Return the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

approaches.name: Sliding window + last seen map
steps: Maintain window [left, right] and a map of char -> last index.
Expand right; if char seen in window, move left past last index.
Update max length with window size.
Return max length after scanning.
approaches.analogy: Like a moving spotlight on a stage — expand right, but jump left past the last repeat of any character.

problems.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
problems.summary: Return the longest substring length with at most k character replacements.

approaches.name: Max window with at most k replacements
steps: Expand right pointer, tracking char frequencies in window.
Let maxFreq be the highest count of any single char in window.
While window size - maxFreq > k, shrink from left.
Track maximum valid window size.
approaches.analogy: Like a spell-checker with k free edits — stretch the window as long as (length − most common char) ≤ k.

approaches.name: Max window with frequency map
steps: Use a frequency map for the current window.
Track the most frequent character count in the window.
Shrink left while replacements needed exceed k.
Return the largest window size seen.
approaches.analogy: Like a spell-checker with k free edits — track letter counts and stretch while replacements stay within budget.

problems.title: Permutation in String
problems.summary: Return true if s2 contains a permutation of s1 as a substring.

approaches.name: Fixed window frequency match
steps: Count character frequencies in s1.
Slide a window of len(s1) over s2, updating counts.
Compare window counts to s1 counts each step.
Return true on first match, else false.
approaches.analogy: Like sliding a fixed-size stencil over text — check if the letter counts inside match the target pattern.

problems.title: Minimum Window Substring
problems.summary: Return the minimum window substring of s containing all chars of t.

approaches.name: Expand/shrink with need counts
steps: Count required chars in t; track how many needs are satisfied.
Expand right until all needs are met.
Shrink left while still valid, recording minimum window.
Return the shortest valid substring found.
approaches.analogy: Like covering all required toppings on a pizza — grow the slice until every topping appears, then trim the excess.

problems.title: Sliding Window Maximum
problems.summary: Return the maximum value in each sliding window of size k.

approaches.name: Monotonic deque
steps: Maintain a deque of indices with decreasing values.
Push right index; pop back while value <= nums[right].
Pop front when index leaves the window.
Record nums[deque front] once window size reaches k.
approaches.analogy: Like a line of people where shorter ones in back get kicked out — only candidates who might be tallest in the window stay.

problems.title: Valid Parentheses
problems.summary: Return true if the string has valid matching parentheses.

approaches.name: Stack matching
steps: Push opening brackets onto a stack.
On closing bracket, pop and verify it matches.
Return false on mismatch or empty stack pop.
Return true if stack is empty at end.
approaches.analogy: Like nesting boxes — push opens, pop closes; a mismatch or leftover open means invalid nesting.

problems.title: Min Stack
problems.summary: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and getMin in O(1).

approaches.name: Two stacks
steps: Maintain a main stack and a min stack.
On push, push to main; push to min if value <= current min.
On pop, pop both if top of main equals top of min.
getMin returns top of min stack.
approaches.analogy: Like a regular stack plus a shadow stack that mirrors the minimum — every push updates both, every pop removes from both.

approaches.name: Single stack with pairs
steps: Store pairs of (value, currentMin) on one stack.
On push, update currentMin and push the pair.
On pop, remove the top pair.
getMin returns currentMin from top pair.
approaches.analogy: Like storing (value, currentMin) pairs on one stack — each entry remembers the minimum at that depth.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
s = MinStack()
s.push(-2)
s.push(0)
s.push(-3)
assert s.getMin() == -3
s.pop()
assert s.top() == 0
assert s.getMin() == -2

tests.label: Test 2
tests.script:
s = MinStack()
s.push(2)
s.push(1)
assert s.getMin() == 1
s.pop()
assert s.top() == 2
assert s.getMin() == 2

tests.label: Test 3
tests.script:
s = MinStack()
s.push(5)
s.push(5)
s.pop()
assert s.top() == 5

problems.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
problems.summary: Evaluate the value of a Reverse Polish Notation expression.

approaches.name: Stack evaluation
steps: Iterate tokens left to right.
Push numbers onto a stack.
On operator, pop two operands, apply op, push result.
Return the final stack value.
approaches.analogy: Like a calculator that waits for numbers — operators pop the last two operands, compute, and push the result back.

problems.title: Daily Temperatures
problems.summary: For each day, return how many days until a warmer temperature.

approaches.name: Monotonic decreasing stack
steps: Iterate indices with a stack of unresolved days.
While current temp > stack top temp, pop and set wait = i - popped.
Push current index onto stack.
Remaining stack entries have answer 0.
approaches.analogy: Like waiting for warmer weather — each day asks the stack of cooler days 'how long until someone warmer shows up?'

problems.title: Car Fleet
problems.summary: Count how many car fleets reach the target given positions and speeds.

approaches.name: Sort by position + stack merge
steps: Pair each car's position with time to reach target.
Sort pairs by position descending (nearest to target first).
Push times onto stack; pop if top time <= new time (same fleet).
Return stack size as fleet count.
approaches.analogy: Like cars merging on a highway — faster cars behind catch up and form fleets; count the surviving groups.

problems.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
problems.summary: Return the area of the largest rectangle in a histogram.

approaches.name: Monotonic increasing stack
steps: Append a sentinel height 0 to flush the stack.
For each bar, while stack top height > current, pop and compute area.
Area uses popped height and width to current or stack top.
Track and return maximum area.
approaches.analogy: Like building a fence — each bar extends left until a taller bar stops it, giving the width for max rectangle area.

problems.title: Binary Search
problems.summary: Return the index of target in nums, or -1 if not found.

approaches.name: Classic lo/hi binary search
steps: Set lo = 0 and hi = len(nums) - 1.
While lo <= hi, set mid and compare nums[mid] to target.
Return mid on equal; search left or right half otherwise.
Return -1 if search space is exhausted.
approaches.analogy: Like guessing a number in 'hot/cold' — halve the search range each guess until you hit the target.

problems.title: Search a 2D Matrix
problems.summary: Return true if target exists in an m x n matrix sorted row-wise.

approaches.name: Treat as 1D sorted array
steps: Set lo = 0 and hi = m*n - 1.
Map mid to matrix[mid/n][mid%n].
Binary search comparing to target.
Return true on match, false otherwise.
approaches.analogy: Like unrolling a newspaper into one long sorted strip — one binary search covers the whole matrix.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
matrix = [[1, 3, 5, 7], [10, 11, 16, 20], [23, 30, 34, 60]]
assert Solution().searchMatrix(matrix, 3) == True

tests.label: Test 2
tests.script:
matrix = [[1, 3, 5, 7], [10, 11, 16, 20], [23, 30, 34, 60]]
assert Solution().searchMatrix(matrix, 13) == False

problems.title: Koko Eating Bananas
problems.summary: Find the minimum eating speed k so Koko finishes all piles within h hours.

approaches.name: Binary search on eating speed
steps: Binary search k from 1 to max(piles).
For candidate k, compute hours = sum(ceil(pile/k)).
If hours <= h, try smaller k; else try larger k.
Return the smallest feasible k.
approaches.analogy: Like tuning a machine's speed — too slow misses the deadline, too fast wastes power; binary search finds the minimum that works.

problems.title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
problems.summary: Find the minimum element in a rotated sorted array with distinct values.

approaches.name: Compare mid with right
steps: Set lo = 0 and hi = len(nums) - 1.
While lo < hi, compare nums[mid] with nums[hi].
If nums[mid] > nums[hi], min is in right half.
Else min is in left half including mid; return nums[lo].
approaches.analogy: Like finding the lowest point on a rotated hill — compare the middle to the right end to know which half contains the minimum.

problems.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
problems.summary: Search for target in a rotated sorted array with distinct values.

approaches.name: Identify sorted half
steps: Binary search with lo/hi pointers.
Determine which half [lo,mid] or [mid,hi] is sorted.
If target lies in sorted half, search there; else search other half.
Return index on match or -1 if not found.
approaches.analogy: Like searching a rotated bookshelf — one half is always properly sorted, so pick that half if the target fits.

problems.title: Time Based Key-Value Store
problems.summary: Design a time-based key-value store with get(key, timestamp).

approaches.name: Hash map + binary search timestamps
steps: Store key -> list of (timestamp, value) pairs.
On set, append (timestamp, value) to key's list.
On get, binary search largest timestamp <= query.
Return corresponding value or empty string.
approaches.analogy: Like a library catalog with dated editions — find the key, then binary search timestamps for the latest ≤ query time.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
tm = TimeMap()
tm.set("foo", "bar", 1)
assert tm.get("foo", 1) == "bar"
assert tm.get("foo", 3) == "bar"

tests.label: Test 2
tests.script:
tm = TimeMap()
tm.set("foo", "bar", 1)
tm.set("foo", "bar2", 4)
assert tm.get("foo", 4) == "bar2"
assert tm.get("foo", 5) == "bar2"

tests.label: Test 3
tests.script:
tm = TimeMap()
tm.set("love", "high", 10)
tm.set("love", "low", 20)
assert tm.get("love", 5) == ""
assert tm.get("love", 10) == "high"
assert tm.get("love", 15) == "high"
assert tm.get("love", 20) == "low"

problems.title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
problems.summary: Return the median of two sorted arrays nums1 and nums2.

approaches.name: Binary search partition
steps: Ensure nums1 is the shorter array; binary search its partition i.
Set j = (m+n+1)/2 - i for nums2's partition.
Compare cross-partition maxes and mins to validate partition.
Return median from left/right max values of valid partition.
approaches.analogy: Like splitting two sorted decks at a cut point — the cut defines left/right halves whose medians meet in the middle.

problems.title: Reverse Linked List
problems.summary: Reverse a singly linked list.

approaches.name: Iterative reversal
steps: Initialize prev = null and curr = head.
Save next, set curr.next = prev, advance prev and curr.
Repeat until curr is null.
Return prev as new head.
approaches.analogy: Like flipping a chain of paper clips — three pointers walk the list, reversing one link at a time.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
def list_to_ll(arr):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    cur = dummy
    for v in arr:
        cur.next = ListNode(v)
        cur = cur.next
    return dummy.next

def ll_to_list(head):
    out = []
    while head:
        out.append(head.val)
        head = head.next
    return out

assert ll_to_list(Solution().reverseList(list_to_ll([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]))) == [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]

problems.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
problems.summary: Merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted list.

approaches.name: Dummy head merge
steps: Create dummy node and a tail pointer.
Compare list1 and list2 heads; attach smaller to tail.
Advance the chosen list and tail.
Attach remaining nodes; return dummy.next.
approaches.analogy: Like weaving two sorted queues into one line — always attach the smaller front node and advance that list.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
def list_to_ll(arr):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    cur = dummy
    for v in arr:
        cur.next = ListNode(v)
        cur = cur.next
    return dummy.next

def ll_to_list(head):
    out = []
    while head:
        out.append(head.val)
        head = head.next
    return out

assert ll_to_list(Solution().mergeTwoLists(list_to_ll([1, 2, 4]), list_to_ll([1, 3, 4]))) == [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4]

problems.title: Reorder List
problems.summary: Reorder list L0->L1->...->Ln to L0->Ln->L1->Ln-1->...

approaches.name: Find mid + reverse + merge
steps: Find middle with slow/fast pointers.
Reverse the second half of the list.
Merge first and reversed second halves alternately.
Return the reordered list head.
approaches.analogy: Like folding a list in half — find the middle, reverse the back half, then zip the two halves together.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
def list_to_ll(arr):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    cur = dummy
    for v in arr:
        cur.next = ListNode(v)
        cur = cur.next
    return dummy.next

def ll_to_list(head):
    out = []
    while head:
        out.append(head.val)
        head = head.next
    return out

head = list_to_ll([1, 2, 3, 4])
Solution().reorderList(head)
assert ll_to_list(head) == [1, 4, 2, 3]

tests.label: Test 2
tests.script:
head = list_to_ll([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
Solution().reorderList(head)
assert ll_to_list(head) == [1, 5, 2, 4, 3]

tests.label: Test 3
tests.script:
head = list_to_ll([1])
Solution().reorderList(head)
assert ll_to_list(head) == [1]

problems.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
problems.summary: Remove the nth node from the end of a linked list.

approaches.name: Fast/slow two pointers
steps: Advance fast pointer n steps ahead.
Move both fast and slow until fast reaches the end.
Remove slow.next (node after nth-from-end).
Handle edge case of removing head with dummy node.
approaches.analogy: Like a tortoise and hare — when the fast pointer is n steps ahead, slow is at the node to remove.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
def list_to_ll(arr):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    cur = dummy
    for v in arr:
        cur.next = ListNode(v)
        cur = cur.next
    return dummy.next

def ll_to_list(head):
    out = []
    while head:
        out.append(head.val)
        head = head.next
    return out

assert ll_to_list(Solution().removeNthFromEnd(list_to_ll([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]), 2)) == [1, 2, 3, 5]

problems.title: Copy List with Random Pointer
problems.summary: Deep copy a linked list where each node has an extra random pointer.

approaches.name: Hash map old to new
steps: First pass: create copy of each node, map old -> new.
Second pass: set next and random using the map.
Return map[head] as new list head.
approaches.analogy: Like photocopying a scrapbook with sticky notes — map each original page to its clone, then wire up the copies.

approaches.name: Interweave + split
steps: Insert copy of each node right after original.
Set random pointers on interleaved copies.
Split list into original and copied lists.
Return head of copied list.
approaches.analogy: Like weaving duplicate pages between originals, then splitting the interleaved chain into a clean copy.

tests.label: Test 1
Test 3
tests.script:
orig = build_list([[3, None], [3, 0], [3, None]])
copy = Solution().copyRandomList(orig)
assert list_vals(copy) == [3, 3, 3]

problems.title: Add Two Numbers
problems.summary: Add two numbers represented as linked lists (digits in reverse order).

approaches.name: Simulate addition with carry
steps: Traverse both lists while either or carry remains.
Sum digits plus carry, append digit % 10 to result.
Carry = sum / 10 for next position.
Return result list head.
approaches.analogy: Like grade-school addition on paper — add digits, carry the overflow, build the result list.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
def list_to_ll(arr):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    cur = dummy
    for v in arr:
        cur.next = ListNode(v)
        cur = cur.next
    return dummy.next

def ll_to_list(head):
    out = []
    while head:
        out.append(head.val)
        head = head.next
    return out

assert ll_to_list(Solution().addTwoNumbers(list_to_ll([2, 4, 3]), list_to_ll([5, 6, 4]))) == [7, 0, 8]

problems.title: Linked List Cycle
problems.summary: Return true if the linked list has a cycle.

approaches.name: Floyd cycle detection
steps: Set slow and fast to head.
Advance slow 1 step and fast 2 steps each iteration.
If they meet, a cycle exists.
If fast reaches null, no cycle.
approaches.analogy: Like two runners on a track — if the fast one laps the slow one, there's a loop.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
n1 = ListNode(3)
n2 = ListNode(2)
n3 = ListNode(0)
n4 = ListNode(-4)
n1.next = n2
n2.next = n3
n3.next = n4
n4.next = n2
assert Solution().hasCycle(n1) == True

tests.label: Test 3
tests.script:
n1 = ListNode(1)
n2 = ListNode(2)
n1.next = n2
n2.next = n1
assert Solution().hasCycle(n1) == True

problems.title: Find the Duplicate Number
problems.summary: Find the duplicate number in nums where values are in range [1, n].

approaches.name: Floyd cycle on index graph
steps: Treat index i as pointing to nums[i] (implicit linked list).
Find cycle entrance with slow/fast pointers.
Reset one pointer to start; advance both 1 step until they meet.
Meeting point is the duplicate value.
approaches.analogy: Like following treasure-map hops — treat indices as pointers; a cycle reveals the duplicate number.

problems.title: LRU Cache
problems.summary: Design an LRU cache with get and put in O(1).

approaches.name: Hash map + doubly linked list
steps: Map key -> node in a doubly linked list (most recent at head).
On get: move node to head if exists; else return -1.
On put: update or insert at head; evict tail if over capacity.
Maintain capacity invariant after each operation.
approaches.analogy: Like a VIP lounge with a guest list — hash map for instant lookup, linked list for eviction order.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
cache = LRUCache(2)
cache.put(1, 1)
cache.put(2, 2)
assert cache.get(1) == 1
cache.put(3, 3)
assert cache.get(2) == -1
cache.put(4, 4)
assert cache.get(1) == -1
assert cache.get(3) == 3
assert cache.get(4) == 4

tests.label: Test 2
tests.script:
cache = LRUCache(1)

problems.title: Merge k Sorted Lists
problems.summary: Merge k sorted linked lists into one sorted list.

approaches.name: Min-heap of k heads
steps: Push head of each non-empty list into a min-heap.
Pop smallest node, append to result, push its next if any.
Repeat until heap is empty.
Return merged list head.
approaches.analogy: Like merging k sorted playlists — always pick the smallest current song from the heap of k front tracks.

approaches.name: Divide and conquer merge
steps: Pair up lists and merge each pair recursively.
Repeat until one list remains.
Use two-list merge helper at each step.
Return the final merged head.
approaches.analogy: Like a tournament bracket — merge pairs of lists repeatedly until one sorted list remains.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
def list_to_ll(arr):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    cur = dummy
    for v in arr:
        cur.next = ListNode(v)
        cur = cur.next
    return dummy.next

def ll_to_list(head):
    out = []
    while head:
        out.append(head.val)
        head = head.next
    return out

lists = [list_to_ll([1, 4, 5]), list_to_ll([1, 3, 4]), list_to_ll([2, 6])]
assert ll_to_list(Solution().mergeKLists(lists)) == [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6]

problems.title: Reverse Nodes in k-Group
problems.summary: Reverse nodes of a linked list k at a time.

approaches.name: Iterative k-group reversal
steps: Count nodes to check if k nodes remain.
Reverse exactly k nodes in place.
Connect reversed segment to rest of list.
Repeat until fewer than k nodes remain.
approaches.analogy: Like flipping cards in batches of k — reverse each group in place, then stitch the batches together.

problems.title: Invert Binary Tree
problems.summary: Invert a binary tree (swap left and right children at every node).

approaches.name: Recursive DFS swap
steps: Base case: return null if root is null.
Swap root.left and root.right.
Recursively invert left and right subtrees.
Return root.
approaches.analogy: Like flipping a family tree photo — swap left and right children at every node, top to bottom.

problems.title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
problems.summary: Return the maximum depth (height) of a binary tree.

approaches.name: Recursive max depth
steps: If root is null, return 0.
Compute leftDepth = maxDepth(left).
Compute rightDepth = maxDepth(right).
Return 1 + max(leftDepth, rightDepth).
approaches.analogy: Like measuring a tree from root to deepest leaf — depth is 1 plus the taller of the two subtrees.

tests.label: Test 1
tests.script:
def list_to_tree(arr):
    if not arr:
        return None
    root = TreeNode(arr[0])
    queue = [root]
    i = 1
    while queue and i < len(arr):
        node = queue.pop(0)
        if i < len(arr) and arr[i] is not None:
            node.left = TreeNode(arr[i])
            queue.append(node.left)
        i += 1
        if i < len(arr) and arr[i] is not None:
            node.right = TreeNode(arr[i])
            queue.append(node.right)
        i += 1
    return root

assert Solution().maxDepth(list_to_tree([3, 9, 20, None, None, 15, 7])) == 3

problems.title: Diameter of Binary Tree
problems.summary: Return the diameter (longest path between any two nodes) of a binary tree.

approaches.name: DFS height with global max
steps: Post-order DFS returns height of subtree.
At each node, update global max with leftHeight + rightHeight.
Return 1 + max(leftHeight, rightHeight) to parent.
Return global max after traversal.
approaches.analogy: Like measuring each node's reach — the diameter through a node is left height + right height.

problems.title: Balanced Binary Tree
problems.summary: Return true if the binary tree is height-balanced.

approaches.name: Post-order height check
steps: DFS returns height, or -1 if unbalanced.
If abs(leftHeight - rightHeight) > 1, return -1.
Propagate -1 up if any child is unbalanced.
Return true if final height is not -1.
approaches.analogy: Like checking a mobile's balance — if any side hangs too low, the whole structure is unbalanced.

problems.title: Same Tree
problems.summary: Return true if two binary trees are structurally identical with same values.

approaches.name: Recursive compare
steps: If both null, return true; if one null, return false.
If values differ, return false.
Return isSameTree(left) && isSameTree(right).
approaches.analogy: Like diffing two family trees — same shape, same values at every node, or they're different.

If isSameTree(root, subRoot), return true.

problems.title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST
problems.summary: Find the lowest common ancestor of two nodes in a BST.

approaches.name: BST property walk
steps: Start at root.
If both values < root.val, go left.
If both values > root.val, go right.
Otherwise root is the LCA; return it.
approaches.analogy: Like a phone book — if both names are before or after the current page, go that direction; otherwise you found the split point.

problems.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
problems.summary: Return level-order traversal of a binary tree (BFS by level).

approaches.name: BFS queue by level
steps: Initialize queue with root.
While queue not empty, process all nodes at current level.
Collect values into a level list, append to result.
Enqueue children of current level nodes.
approaches.analogy: Like ripples in a pond — process one ring of nodes at a time, enqueue the next ring.

problems.title: Binary Tree Right Side View
problems.summary: Return the values visible from the right side of a binary tree.

approaches.name: BFS last node per level
steps: BFS level by level with a queue.
For each level, record every node value.
Keep only the last value of each level.
Return the collected right-side values.
approaches.analogy: Like watching a parade from the right — the last person in each row is what you see.

approaches.name: DFS right-first preorder
steps: DFS visiting right child before left.
Track current depth; first visit at each depth is visible.
Record node value when depth exceeds max depth seen.
Return recorded values in order.
approaches.analogy: Like peeking around corners right-first — the first node you discover at each depth is the rightmost visible one.

steps: DFS with current path maximum.
If node.val >= pathMax, increment good count.
Recurse to children with updated max(pathMax, node.val).
Return total good nodes.
approaches.analogy: Like a hero's journey — a node is 'good' if no stronger enemy appeared on the path from the start.

problems.title: Validate Binary Search Tree
problems.summary: Return true if a binary tree is a valid BST.

approaches.name: In-order or min/max bounds
steps: Pass down (min, max) bounds for each node.
Return false if node.val <= min or >= max.
Recurse left with (min, node.val) and right with (node.val, max).
Return true if all nodes satisfy bounds.
approaches.analogy: Like a thermostat range — each node must stay strictly between its parent's lower and upper limits.

steps: Perform in-order DFS (left, node, right).
Increment counter on each node visit.
Return node value when counter reaches k.
approaches.analogy: Like reading a BST like a sorted list — visit left, then node, then right; the kth visit is the answer.

approaches.name: Iterative stack in-order
steps: Push left spine onto stack from root.
Pop, visit, then push left spine of right child.
Return value on kth visit.
approaches.analogy: Like reading a BST with bookmarks — push left spine, pop and visit, then dive into the right subtree.

problems.summary: Build a binary tree from preorder and inorder traversal arrays.

approaches.name: Recursive with hash map index
steps: Map each inorder value to its index.
Preorder root is first element; find its inorder index.
Recursively build left subtree from left inorder segment.
Recursively build right subtree from right inorder segment.
approaches.analogy: Like assembling a tree from a preorder blueprint — the inorder index tells you where to split left and right.

approaches.name: Post-order max gain
steps: Post-order DFS returns max gain from node downward.
At each node, compute path through node = leftGain + rightGain + val.
Update global max with this path sum.
Return node.val + max(leftGain, rightGain, 0) to parent.
approaches.analogy: Like a toll road through a tree — the best path through a node combines the best gains from both subtrees.

problems.title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
problems.summary: Serialize and deserialize a binary tree to/from a string.

approaches.name: Preorder with null markers
steps: Serialize: preorder DFS, write 'null' for empty nodes.
Deserialize: read tokens, build node or return null.
Recurse left then right for each node.
Return root of reconstructed tree.
approaches.analogy: Like dictating a tree over the phone — say each node, and 'null' for empty branches.

approaches.name: BFS level serialization
steps: Serialize: BFS queue, output values including nulls.
Deserialize: read queue tokens level by level.
Attach left and right children from queue.
Return root when tree is built.
approaches.analogy: Like reading a tree row by row, including empty seats — rebuild level by level from the queue.

problems.title: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
problems.summary: Design a class that returns the kth largest element in a stream.

steps: Maintain a min-heap of the k largest elements seen.
On add: push val; if size > k, pop smallest.
Return heap top as kth largest.
Heap always holds the k largest values.

problems.title: Last Stone Weight
problems.summary: Repeatedly smash the two heaviest stones; return the last stone weight.

approaches.name: Max-heap smash simulation
steps: Push all stone weights into a max-heap.
While more than one stone, pop two largest y and x.
If x != y, push y - x back.
Return remaining stone or 0.
approaches.analogy: Like smashing the two heaviest rocks together — repeat until one or none remain.

problems.title: K Closest Points to Origin
problems.summary: Return the k points closest to the origin (0, 0).

approaches.name: Max-heap of size k
steps: Compute squared distance for each point.
Maintain max-heap of size k by distance.
Pop and replace when a closer point is found.
Return all points in the heap.
approaches.analogy: Like keeping the k closest parking spots — evict the farthest whenever a closer one appears.

approaches.name: Quickselect
steps: Partition points by distance around a pivot.
Recursively narrow to the k smallest partition.
Return first k points after partitioning.
Average O(n) selection of k closest.
approaches.analogy: Like partitioning a crowd by height — narrow to the k shortest partition without fully sorting everyone.

problems.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
problems.summary: Find the kth largest element in an unsorted array.

approaches.name: Min-heap of size k
steps: Push elements into min-heap of size k.
If heap exceeds k, pop smallest.
Heap top is the kth largest element.
Return heap top.
approaches.analogy: Like a leaderboard that only keeps the top k — the smallest in the heap is the kth largest overall.

approaches.name: Quickselect
steps: Choose pivot and partition array around it.
Recurse on side containing the kth position.
Return element at index k-1 when partition size is 1.
Average O(n) time selection.
approaches.analogy: Like finding the kth tallest person without ranking everyone — partition and recurse on the right group.

problems.title: Task Scheduler
problems.summary: Find minimum intervals needed to complete all tasks with cooldown n.

approaches.name: Max-heap + idle slots formula
steps: Count task frequencies.
Most frequent task determines frame: (maxFreq-1)*(n+1) + countAtMax.
Answer is max of that formula and total tasks.
Return computed minimum intervals.
approaches.analogy: Like scheduling the busiest task first with mandatory breaks — the most frequent task sets the minimum frame size.

Merge top 10 by time using a min-heap or sorting.
Return the 10 most recent tweet ids.

problems.title: Find Median from Data Stream
problems.summary: Support addNum and findMedian for a stream of integers.

approaches.name: Two heaps (max + min)
steps: Maintain max-heap for lower half and min-heap for upper half.
On addNum, push to appropriate heap and rebalance sizes.
Keep sizes differing by at most 1.
Median is top of max-heap or average of both tops.
approaches.analogy: Like splitting a class into upper and lower halves — the median sits at the boundary between two heaps.

problems.title: Subsets
problems.summary: Return all possible subsets (power set) of nums.

approaches.name: Include/exclude recursion
steps: Start with empty current subset.
At each index, choose to include or exclude nums[i].
On reaching end, append copy of current to result.
Return all collected subsets.
approaches.analogy: Like packing a suitcase — for each item, decide to take it or leave it, exploring both choices.

problems.title: Combination Sum
problems.summary: Return all unique combinations where candidates sum to target (reuse allowed).

approaches.name: Backtrack with reuse
steps: Sort candidates (optional) and backtrack from index.
Include candidate, recurse with same index (reuse).
Exclude candidate, recurse with index+1.
Record combination when sum equals target.
approaches.analogy: Like making change with reusable coins — pick a coin, stay at the same index, backtrack when over target.

problems.title: Permutations
problems.summary: Return all permutations of nums.

approaches.name: Swap-based backtracking
steps: Fix position i and swap with each j >= i.
Recurse on i+1 to build rest of permutation.
Undo swap (backtrack) after recursion.
Collect permutation when i reaches end.
approaches.analogy: Like arranging people in a line by swapping — fix position i, try every person for that spot, undo the swap.

approaches.name: Used array backtracking
steps: Track which indices are used in current permutation.
Try each unused element at current position.
Recurse to fill next position.
Backtrack by unmarking used on return.
approaches.analogy: Like seating guests one by one — try each unused person at the current chair, backtrack when all seats are filled.

problems.title: Subsets II
problems.summary: Return all unique subsets of nums that may contain duplicates.

approaches.name: Sort + skip duplicates
steps: Sort nums to group duplicates.
Backtrack: include or skip each element.
When skipping, skip all equal elements at same level.
Collect subset at each recursion leaf.
approaches.analogy: Like building subsets from sorted beads — skip identical beads at the same decision level to avoid duplicates.

problems.title: Combination Sum II
problems.summary: Return combinations summing to target using each candidate at most once.

approaches.name: Sort + no reuse backtrack
steps: Sort candidates and backtrack from start index.
Include candidate and recurse from i+1.
Skip candidate and recurse from i+1.
Record when running sum equals target.
approaches.analogy: Like picking coins once each from a sorted pile — include or skip, but never reuse the same coin.

problems.title: Word Search
problems.summary: Return true if word exists in the board (adjacent cell path).

approaches.name: DFS backtrack on board
steps: For each cell matching first letter, start DFS.
Mark cell visited, explore 4 neighbors for next char.
Unmark on backtrack.
Return true on full word match.
approaches.analogy: Like tracing a word in a word search — walk adjacent cells, mark visited, undo on dead ends.

problems.title: Palindrome Partitioning
problems.summary: Return all ways to partition s such that every part is a palindrome.

approaches.name: Backtrack with palindrome check
steps: Try every end index for next partition from start.
If substring is palindrome, add to path and recurse.
Remove from path on backtrack.
Collect path when start reaches end of s.
approaches.analogy: Like cutting a string into palindrome slices — try every cut point, backtrack if the piece isn't a palindrome.

problems.title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
problems.summary: Return all letter combinations from a phone digit string.

approaches.name: Digit-to-letters backtrack
steps: Map each digit to its phone letters.
Build current string character by character.
Recurse to next digit index.
Add completed string to result.
approaches.analogy: Like dialing old phone keys — each digit opens a branch of possible letter combinations.

problems.title: N-Queens
problems.summary: Return all distinct solutions to the n-queens puzzle.

approaches.name: Column/diag set backtrack
steps: Place queen row by row in valid columns.
Track used columns and diagonals (row±col).
Backtrack by removing queen from current row.
Record board when all rows are placed.
approaches.analogy: Like placing queens on a chessboard row by row — track attacked columns and diagonals, backtrack on conflict.

python.solution:
class Solution:
    def solveNQueens(self, n):
        cols = set()
        pos_diag = set()
        neg_diag = set()
        board = [['.'] * n for _ in range(n)]
        result = []

        def dfs(r):
            if r == n:
                result.append(["".join(row) for row in board])
                return
            for c in range(n):
                if c in cols or (r + c) in pos_diag or (r - c) in neg_diag:
                    continue
                cols.add(c)
                pos_diag.add(r + c)
                neg_diag.add(r - c)
                board[r][c] = 'Q'
                dfs(r + 1)
                board[r][c] = '.'
                cols.remove(c)
                pos_diag.remove(r + c)
                neg_diag.remove(r - c)

        dfs(0)
        return result

problems.title: Course Schedule
problems.summary: Return true if all courses can be finished given prerequisites.

approaches.name: Kahn's topological sort
steps: Build graph and indegree array.
Enqueue nodes with indegree 0.
Process queue, decrement neighbor indegrees.
Return true if all nodes were processed.
approaches.analogy: Like taking courses with no prerequisites first — peel off zero-indegree nodes layer by layer.

approaches.name: DFS cycle detection
steps: Build adjacency list from prerequisites.
DFS with states: unvisited, visiting, visited.
If edge leads to visiting node, cycle exists.
Return false on cycle, true if no cycle.
approaches.analogy: Like detecting circular prerequisites — a node still 'in progress' when revisited means a cycle.

problems.title: Course Schedule II
problems.summary: Return a valid course ordering, or empty if impossible.

approaches.name: Kahn's BFS order
steps: Build graph and indegree counts.
BFS queue of indegree-0 courses.
Append to order and reduce neighbor indegrees.
Return order if size equals numCourses, else [].
approaches.analogy: Like scheduling tasks — finish courses with no remaining prerequisites first, in BFS order.

approaches.name: DFS post-order
steps: DFS each unvisited node with cycle detection.
Append node to order after visiting all descendants.
Reverse order at end for topological sort.
Return [] if cycle detected.
approaches.analogy: Like finishing dependencies before the course itself — reverse post-order gives a valid schedule.

problems.title: Redundant Connection
problems.summary: Find the edge that can be removed to make a tree (undirected graph).

approaches.name: Union-Find last redundant edge
steps: Process edges in order with Union-Find.
If both endpoints already share a parent, edge is redundant.
Return the last such edge found.
Union otherwise to connect components.
approaches.analogy: Like wiring a network — the first edge connecting already-connected nodes is redundant.

problems.title: Number of Connected Components
problems.summary: Return the number of connected components in an undirected graph.

approaches.name: Union-Find count
steps: Initialize each node as its own parent.
Union endpoints for each edge.
Count distinct root parents.
Return component count.
approaches.analogy: Like counting friend groups — merge connected people and count distinct group leaders.

approaches.name: DFS count components
steps: Build adjacency list.
For each unvisited node, DFS and increment count.
Mark visited during DFS.
Return total component count.
approaches.analogy: Like counting isolated islands in a social graph — each unvisited node starts a new DFS group.

problems.title: Graph Valid Tree
problems.summary: Return true if edges form a valid tree (connected, no cycles).

approaches.name: Union-Find no cycle + connected
steps: Union-Find on edges; return false if union finds same parent.
After all edges, check exactly one component (n-1 edges).
Valid tree needs n-1 edges and full connectivity.
Return true only if both hold.
approaches.analogy: Like checking if wires form a tree — n−1 connections with no cycles and everyone reachable.

approaches.name: BFS/DFS connectivity
steps: Build adjacency from edges.
BFS/DFS from node 0; count visited nodes.
Check edges.length == n-1 and visited == n.
Return true if connected acyclic structure.
approaches.analogy: Like checking if a road network is one connected tree — exactly n−1 roads and all nodes reachable.

problems.title: Word Ladder
problems.summary: Return the length of shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord.

approaches.name: BFS shortest transformation
steps: BFS queue with (word, level).
Try changing each char to a-z; check wordList set.
Enqueue unvisited valid words.
Return level when endWord is reached, or 0.
approaches.analogy: Like word ladder rungs — BFS finds the shortest chain of one-letter changes to the target.

problems.title: Reconstruct Itinerary
problems.summary: Reconstruct the lexicographically smallest valid itinerary from tickets.

approaches.name: Hierholzer Eulerian path
steps: Build graph; sort each node's neighbors.
DFS consuming edges (post-order stack).
Append airport after exhausting outgoing edges.
Reverse result for itinerary order.
approaches.analogy: Like walking every flight route exactly once — consume edges in DFS post-order, then reverse for the itinerary.

approaches.name: DFS post-order
steps: Sort adjacency lists lexicographically.
DFS from JFK, removing used edges.
Push airport to route after visiting neighbors.
Reverse collected route at end.
approaches.analogy: Like using every ticket exactly once — DFS eats edges and builds the route in reverse order.

problems.title: Min Cost to Connect All Points
problems.summary: Return min cost to connect all points into one network (MST).

approaches.name: Prim's MST
steps: Start from any point; track min edge cost to tree.
Repeatedly add cheapest outside point.
Use min-heap or array scan for next edge.
Sum edge costs until all points connected.
approaches.analogy: Like growing a cable network — always connect the cheapest outside point to the existing tree.

approaches.name: Kruskal with Union-Find
steps: Sort all edges by Manhattan distance.
Union-Find merge cheapest edges first.
Add cost when union connects different components.
Stop when n-1 edges are added.
approaches.analogy: Like building roads cheapest-first — merge components with the smallest edges until all are connected.

problems.title: Network Delay Time
problems.summary: Return time for signal from node k to reach all nodes, or -1.

approaches.name: Dijkstra from source
steps: Min-heap of (time, node) starting at k.
Relax edges: update dist if shorter path found.
Skip stale heap entries.
Return max dist or -1 if any node unreachable.
approaches.analogy: Like GPS routing — always relax the closest unvisited node to find shortest times to everyone.

problems.title: Swim in Rising Water
problems.summary: Return minimum time to reach bottom-right cell as water level rises.

approaches.name: Binary search on max height
steps: Binary search max elevation needed on path.
Check feasibility with BFS/DFS allowing cells <= mid.
Minimize mid until path from top-left to bottom-right exists.
Return smallest feasible max height.
approaches.analogy: Like finding the lowest water level that still connects start to finish — binary search the max elevation on the path.

approaches.name: Dijkstra on grid
steps: Min-heap of (maxHeightSoFar, row, col) from (0,0).
Track best max-height to reach each cell.
Relax 4 neighbors with updated path max.
Return dist at bottom-right when reached.
approaches.analogy: Like swimming through rising water — track the worst height on your path and always take the easiest next step.

problems.title: Alien Dictionary
problems.summary: Return alien dictionary character order from sorted words, or '' if invalid.

approaches.name: Build graph + topological sort
steps: Compare adjacent words to find first differing char edge.
Detect invalid cases (prefix longer word before shorter).
Topological sort the char graph.
Return order string or '' on cycle.
approaches.analogy: Like deducing alphabet order from a dictionary — adjacent word pairs give letter precedence edges.

problems.title: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops
problems.summary: Find cheapest price from src to dst with at most k stops.

approaches.name: Bellman-Ford K iterations
steps: Copy dist array each of k+1 relaxation rounds.
Relax all edges using previous round's distances.
Prevents using more than k+1 edges.
Return dist[dst] or -1 if unreachable.
approaches.analogy: Like budgeted travel — relax all routes exactly k+1 times, copying distances each round.

approaches.name: BFS with (node, stops, cost)
steps: BFS/queue states: (node, stops used, cost).
Prune if stops > k or cost not improved.
Relax neighbors with stops+1.
Track minimum cost to dst within k stops.
approaches.analogy: Like finding the cheapest flight with a layover limit — BFS tracks stops used and prunes over-budget paths.

problems.title: Climbing Stairs
problems.summary: Return the number of distinct ways to climb n stairs (1 or 2 steps).

approaches.name: Fibonacci DP
steps: Set dp[1]=1 and dp[2]=2.
For i from 3 to n: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2].
Use two variables instead of full array.
Return dp[n].
approaches.analogy: Like climbing stairs two ways at a time — ways to reach step n = ways to (n−1) + ways to (n−2).

problems.title: Min Cost Climbing Stairs
problems.summary: Return minimum cost to reach top of stairs (can start at step 0 or 1).

approaches.name: Bottom-up min of prev two
steps: dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]).
Initialize dp[0]=cost[0], dp[1]=cost[1].
Iterate i from 2 to top index.
Return min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]) for final step.
approaches.analogy: Like choosing the cheaper of the last two steps before paying for the current one — minimize total stair cost.

problems.title: House Robber
problems.summary: Return maximum money robbed from non-adjacent houses on a street.

approaches.name: Rob/skip DP
steps: Track robPrev and skipPrev as you iterate.
At each house: newRob = skipPrev + nums[i].
newSkip = max(robPrev, skipPrev).
Return max(robPrev, skipPrev) at end.
approaches.analogy: Like robbing houses on a street — take this house plus best from two doors back, or skip and keep the previous best.

problems.title: House Robber II
problems.summary: Return max money robbed when houses are arranged in a circle.

approaches.name: Two linear passes skip ends
steps: Rob houses 0..n-2 with linear house robber DP.
Rob houses 1..n-1 with same DP.
Take max of both results.
Handles first/last adjacency constraint.
approaches.analogy: Like robbing a circular street — run the robber twice, skipping either the first or last house.

problems.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
problems.summary: Return the longest palindromic substring of s.

approaches.name: Expand around center
steps: For each index, expand as odd-length center.
For each pair, expand as even-length center.
Track longest palindrome start and length.
Return substring from best range.
approaches.analogy: Like checking a palindrome by growing outward from each center — odd and even centers both get a try.

problems.title: Palindromic Substrings
problems.summary: Return the count of palindromic substrings in s.

approaches.name: Expand around center
steps: For each center, expand while chars match.
Count each valid palindrome found during expansion.
Try odd and even centers.
Return total count.
approaches.analogy: Like counting palindromes by expanding from every center — each match adds one to the count.

problems.title: Decode Ways
problems.summary: Return the number of ways to decode a digit string to letters.

approaches.name: Linear DP ways[i]
steps: ways[0]=1; ways[1] based on first one/two digits valid.
For i from 2: add ways[i-1] if single digit valid.
Add ways[i-2] if two-digit substring valid.
Return ways[n].
approaches.analogy: Like decoding a phone keypad — each position adds ways from a single digit or a valid two-digit chunk.

problems.title: Coin Change
problems.summary: Return the fewest number of coins needed to make amount, or -1.

approaches.name: Unbounded knapsack DP
steps: Initialize dp[0]=0, others to infinity.
For each coin, update dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a-coin]+1).
Iterate amounts from coin value upward.
Return dp[amount] or -1 if unreachable.
approaches.analogy: Like making change with unlimited coins — for each amount, try every coin and keep the minimum count.

problems.title: Maximum Product Subarray
problems.summary: Return the maximum product of any contiguous subarray.

approaches.name: Track max and min product
steps: Track curMax and curMin ending at each index.
Update both considering nums[i] and nums[i]*prev.
Swap max/min when nums[i] is negative.
Return global maximum product.
approaches.analogy: Like tracking best and worst streaks — a negative number flips min into max, so track both.

problems.title: Word Break
problems.summary: Return true if s can be segmented into dictionary words.

approaches.name: DP reachable[i]
steps: reachable[0] = true.
For each start where reachable[start], try each word.
If s[start:start+len] in dict, mark reachable[start+len].
Return reachable[len(s)].
approaches.analogy: Like breaking a sentence into dictionary words — if you can reach position i and the next word is valid, mark i+len reachable.

problems.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
problems.summary: Return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

approaches.name: Patience sorting / tails
steps: Maintain tails array of smallest tail per length.
For each num, binary search position in tails.
Replace or extend tails accordingly.
Return tails length.
approaches.analogy: Like the card game Patience — each new card replaces the smallest pile top it can beat, and pile count is LIS length.

problems.title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
problems.summary: Return true if nums can be partitioned into two equal-sum subsets.

approaches.name: 0/1 knapsack DP
steps: If total sum odd, return false.
Target = sum/2; dp[0]=true.
For each num, update dp backward from target.
Return dp[target].
approaches.analogy: Like packing a backpack to exactly half capacity — each number is used once to hit the target sum.

problems.title: Unique Paths
problems.summary: Return number of unique paths from top-left to bottom-right (only down/right).

approaches.name: Grid DP bottom-up
steps: Initialize first row and column to 1.
Fill grid row by row.
Return dp[m-1][n-1].
approaches.analogy: Like walking a grid only right or down — each cell's paths = paths from above + paths from the left.

approaches.name: Combinatorics C(m+n-2,m-1)
steps: Total moves = (m-1)+(n-1).
Choose (m-1) down moves from total.
Compute binomial coefficient.
Return combination value.
approaches.analogy: Like choosing which steps are 'down' in a fixed walk — pure math counts paths without a grid.

problems.title: Longest Common Subsequence
problems.summary: Return the length of the longest common subsequence of text1 and text2.

approaches.name: 2D DP table
steps: dp[i][j] = LCS length of text1[0:i] and text2[0:j].
If chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1.
Else dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
Return dp[m][n].
approaches.analogy: Like aligning two strings on graph paper — match extends the diagonal, mismatch takes the best of up or left.

approaches.name: Space-optimized 1D
steps: Use 1D dp array over text2 length.
Iterate text1; update dp right-to-left.
Same recurrence as 2D version.
Return dp[n] after processing.
approaches.analogy: Like aligning two strings with one row of sticky notes — same logic, less memory.

problems.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
problems.summary: Return max profit with buy/sell and a cooldown day after each sell.

approaches.name: State machine DP (hold/sold/rest)
steps: Track hold, sold, rest max profits each day.
hold = max(hold, rest - price).
sold = hold + price; rest = max(rest, sold).
Return max(sold, rest) at end.
approaches.analogy: Like stock trading with a cooldown — three moods (holding, just sold, resting) govern each day's best profit.

problems.title: Coin Change 2
problems.summary: Return the number of combinations of coins that make up amount.

approaches.name: Unbounded knapsack ways
steps: ways[0] = 1.
For each coin, add ways[a-coin] to ways[a].
Iterate amounts ascending per coin.
Return ways[amount].
approaches.analogy: Like counting coin combinations — each coin can be reused, and ways build up amount by amount.

problems.title: Target Sum
problems.summary: Return number of ways to assign +/- to nums to reach target sum.

approaches.name: Subset sum DP
steps: Transform to subset sum: (sum + target) must be even.
Count subsets summing to (sum + target) / 2.
Standard 0/1 knapsack count DP.
Return subset count or 0 if impossible.
approaches.analogy: Like assigning +/- signs — count subsets that sum to half of (total + target).

problems.title: Interleaving String
problems.summary: Return true if s3 is formed by interleaving s1 and s2.

approaches.name: 2D DP match s1/s2
steps: dp[i][j] = can s1[0:i] and s2[0:j] form s3[0:i+j].
Match next char from s1 or s2 to s3.
Base: empty strings match empty prefix.
Return dp[m][n].
approaches.analogy: Like weaving two threads into a third — each char of s3 must come from the next char of s1 or s2.

approaches.name: Space-optimized 1D
steps: Use 1D dp over s2 length.
Update row for each prefix of s1.
Same interleaving recurrence.
Return dp[n] after full s1 processed.
approaches.analogy: Like weaving two threads with one row of notes — same interleaving check, compressed memory.

problems.title: Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix
problems.summary: Return length of longest strictly increasing path in a matrix.

approaches.name: DFS + memoization
steps: DFS from each cell with memo cache.
Explore 4 neighbors strictly greater than current.
memo[r][c] = 1 + max DFS neighbor length.
Return max memo value over all cells.
approaches.analogy: Like hiking only uphill — memoize the longest trail from each cell to avoid recomputing paths.

problems.title: Distinct Subsequences
problems.summary: Return count of distinct subsequences of s that equal t.

approaches.name: 2D DP count matches
steps: dp[i][j] = ways s[0:i] matches t[0:j].
If s[i-1]==t[j-1], add dp[i-1][j-1] to dp[i-1][j].
Always carry dp[i-1][j] forward.
Return dp[m][n].
approaches.analogy: Like counting ways to spell t from s — each char match adds paths from the shorter prefixes.

approaches.name: Space-optimized 1D
steps: 1D dp over t length.
Update right-to-left for each s char.
Same subsequence counting logic.
Return dp[n].
approaches.analogy: Like counting subsequence matches with one row — update right-to-left for each char of s.

problems.title: Edit Distance
problems.summary: Return minimum edit operations to convert word1 to word2.

approaches.name: Levenshtein DP
steps: dp[i][j] = edits between word1[0:i] and word2[0:j].
On match, dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1].
Else 1 + min(insert, delete, replace).
Return dp[m][n].
approaches.analogy: Like spell-check distance — insert, delete, or replace to transform one word into another, minimizing edits.

approaches.name: Space-optimized 1D
steps: Two rows or 1D rolling array.
Iterate word1 updating dp over word2.
Same min-edit recurrence.
Return final dp[n].
approaches.analogy: Like spell-check with one row of costs — same edit distance, rolling memory.

problems.title: Burst Balloons
problems.summary: Return maximum coins collected by bursting balloons optimally.

approaches.name: Interval DP
steps: Pad array with 1 at both ends.
dp[left][right] = max coins for open interval (left, right).
Try each last balloon k in interval.
dp[left][right] = max of dp[left][k] + val + dp[k][right].
approaches.analogy: Like bursting balloons for max coins — try each balloon as the last to pop in an interval, combining sub-results.

problems.title: Regular Expression Matching
problems.summary: Return true if s matches pattern p with '.' and '*' wildcards.

approaches.name: 2D DP with .* cases
steps: dp[i][j] = match s[0:i] with p[0:j].
Handle '*' by zero occurrences or repeat prior char.
Handle '.' as any single char match.
Return dp[m][n].
approaches.analogy: Like regex matching with wildcards — '*' means zero or more of the prior char, '.' matches anything.

problems.title: Maximum Subarray
problems.summary: Return the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray.

approaches.name: Kadane's algorithm
steps: Track curSum and maxSum.
curSum = max(nums[i], curSum + nums[i]).
Update maxSum with curSum each step.
Return maxSum.
approaches.analogy: Like finding the best streak — restart the sum when it goes negative, track the all-time maximum.

problems.title: Jump Game
problems.summary: Return true if you can reach the last index from index 0.

approaches.name: Track farthest reachable
steps: Track farthest index reachable so far.
If i > farthest, return false.
Update farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]).
Return true if farthest >= last index.
approaches.analogy: Like jumping on stones — track the farthest landing spot; if you can't reach index i, you're stuck.

approaches.name: Backward greedy
steps: Set goal = last index.
Scan backward from n-2.
If i + nums[i] >= goal, move goal to i.
Return true if goal reaches 0.
approaches.analogy: Like working backward from the finish — move the goal left whenever a stone can reach it.

problems.title: Jump Game II
problems.summary: Return the minimum number of jumps to reach the last index.

approaches.name: Greedy BFS-like jumps
steps: Track current jump range end and next range end.
Expand next end with i + nums[i] within current range.
Increment jumps when i reaches current end.
Return jumps when end reaches last index.
approaches.analogy: Like minimum hops in a game — each jump covers a range; count jumps when you must extend the range.

problems.title: Gas Station
problems.summary: Return starting gas station index to complete a circuit, or -1.

approaches.name: Single pass total + start
steps: Track total tank and current tank.
If tank goes negative, reset start to i+1.
Accumulate gas - cost each step.
Return start if total >= 0, else -1.
approaches.analogy: Like driving a circular gas route — if you run empty, restart from the next station; if total gas ≥ total cost, a solution exists.

problems.title: Hand of Straights
problems.summary: Return true if hand can be rearranged into groups of consecutive cards.

approaches.name: Sort + hash map groups
steps: Count card frequencies in hash map.
Process cards in sorted order.
For each card, form groupSize consecutive group or return false.
Decrement counts as groups are formed.
approaches.analogy: Like dealing consecutive card runs — sort first, then peel off groupSize consecutive cards from each rank.

problems.title: Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet
problems.summary: Return true if some triplets can be merged to form target triplet.

approaches.name: Track max of needed dims
steps: Track max values seen for each target dimension.
Ignore triplet if any value exceeds target.
If all three dims reach target max, return true.
Return false after scanning all triplets.
approaches.analogy: Like collecting upgrade tokens — ignore any triplet that overshoots target; win when all three dimensions are covered.

problems.title: Partition Labels
problems.summary: Partition string so each part's chars appear only in that part.

approaches.name: Last occurrence greedy partition
steps: Record last index of each character.
Extend end to max last index of chars in current part.
When i reaches end, cut part and reset start.
Return lengths of all parts.
approaches.analogy: Like cutting a string so each letter stays in one piece — extend each part to the last appearance of its letters.

problems.title: Valid Parenthesis String
problems.summary: Return true if string with '(' ')' '*' can form valid parentheses.

approaches.name: Two-pass open/close balance
steps: Forward pass: treat '*' as '('; track balance >= 0.
Backward pass: treat '*' as ')'; track balance >= 0.
Return true if both passes succeed.
Covers all '*' interpretations.
approaches.analogy: Like checking parentheses with wildcards — scan forward treating '*' as '(', then backward treating '*' as ')'.

approaches.name: Greedy range lo/hi
steps: Track min and max possible open count after each char.
Update range for '(', ')', and '*'.
If lo < 0, shift range up.
Return lo == 0 at end.
approaches.analogy: Like tracking how many open parens '*' could represent — keep a min/max balance range alive after each char.

problems.title: Insert Interval
problems.summary: Insert newInterval into intervals and merge overlapping intervals.

approaches.name: Merge in one pass
steps: Add intervals entirely before newInterval.
Merge overlapping intervals with newInterval.
Append remaining intervals after merged block.
Return result list.
approaches.analogy: Like inserting a new meeting into a calendar — merge overlapping blocks in one sweep.

python.solution:
class Solution:
    def insert(self, intervals, newInterval):
        res = []
        i = 0
        n = len(intervals)
        while i < n and intervals[i][1] < newInterval[0]:
            res.append(intervals[i])
            i += 1
        while i < n and intervals[i][0] <= newInterval[1]:
            newInterval[0] = min(newInterval[0], intervals[i][0])
            newInterval[1] = max(newInterval[1], intervals[i][1])
            i += 1
        res.append(newInterval)
        while i < n:
            res.append(intervals[i])
            i += 1
        return res

problems.title: Merge Intervals
problems.summary: Merge all overlapping intervals.

approaches.name: Sort by start + merge
steps: Sort intervals by start time.
Initialize merged with first interval.
If overlap, extend last end; else append.
Return merged list.
approaches.analogy: Like merging overlapping appointments — sort by start, extend the last block or start a new one.

problems.title: Non-overlapping Intervals
problems.summary: Return minimum intervals to remove so none overlap.

approaches.name: Sort by end + greedy keep
steps: Sort intervals by end time.
Keep interval if start >= prev end.
Else increment remove count (drop overlapping).
Return remove count.
approaches.analogy: Like picking non-overlapping meetings — always keep the one that ends earliest to leave room for more.

problems.title: Meeting Rooms
problems.summary: Return true if a person can attend all meetings (no overlaps).

approaches.name: Sort + check overlap
steps: Sort intervals by start.
Compare each start to previous end.
Return false on overlap.
Return true if no overlaps.
approaches.analogy: Like checking a calendar — sort meetings and flag if any start comes before the previous end.

problems.title: Meeting Rooms II
problems.summary: Return minimum number of rooms needed for all meetings.

approaches.name: Min-heap of end times
steps: Sort meetings by start.
Min-heap stores end times of ongoing meetings.
If start >= heap min, reuse room (pop).
Return heap size as rooms needed.
approaches.analogy: Like assigning conference rooms — reuse a room if the next meeting starts after the earliest ending one.

approaches.name: Sweep line
steps: Create start +1 and end -1 events.
Sort events by time.
Track concurrent meetings with running sum.
Return maximum concurrent count.
approaches.analogy: Like counting people entering/leaving — track concurrent events to find peak overlap.

problems.title: Minimum Interval to Include Each Query
problems.summary: For each query, return smallest interval size containing it, or -1.

approaches.name: Sort queries + sweep with heap
approaches.time: O(n log n + q log q)
steps: Sort intervals by start; sort queries with indices.
Push intervals starting <= query into min-heap by size.
Pop intervals that do not contain query.
Record heap top size or -1 per query.
approaches.analogy: Like answering 'smallest fitting box' questions — sweep intervals in, heap by size, pop ones too small.

problems.summary: Rotate an n x n matrix 90 degrees clockwise in place.

steps: Process concentric layers from outside in.
Rotate four cells in each layer position.
Advance around the layer.
Return rotated matrix.
approaches.analogy: Like rotating an onion ring by ring — swap four corners at each layer position.

approaches.name: Transpose + reverse rows
steps: Transpose matrix over main diagonal.
Reverse each row.
Equivalent to 90° clockwise rotation.
Return result matrix.

problems.summary: Return all elements of an m x n matrix in spiral order.

Traverse right, down, left, up in order.
Shrink boundaries after each direction.
Collect until boundaries cross.

problems.title: Set Matrix Zeroes
problems.summary: Set matrix cells to zero if their row or column contains zero.

approaches.name: First row/col as markers
steps: Use first row and column as zero flags.
Handle first row/col zero separately.
Mark zeros in body using first row/col.
Apply marks to zero rows and columns.

problems.title: Happy Number
problems.summary: Return true if n is a happy number (sum of squared digits reaches 1).

approaches.name: Floyd cycle on digit squares
steps: Define next(n) as sum of squares of digits.
Use slow/fast pointers on next chain.
If fast reaches 1, return true.
If slow meets fast (cycle not 1), return false.
approaches.analogy: Like happy-number chain — if slow and fast meet at 1, it's happy; any other cycle means sad.

problems.title: Plus One
problems.summary: Add one to a large integer represented as digit array.

approaches.name: Simulate carry from end
steps: Start from last digit, add 1 with carry.
Propagate carry left while digit is 9.
If carry remains, prepend 1.

approaches.analogy: Like odometer rollover — add 1 from the right, carry left through 9s, prepend 1 if needed.

problems.title: Pow(x, n)
problems.summary: Compute x raised to the power n.

approaches.name: Fast exponentiation
steps: Handle negative n by inverting result at end.
While n > 0: if n odd, multiply result by x.
Square x and halve n each iteration.
Return result.
approaches.analogy: Like repeated squaring — halve the exponent each step, multiply extra when the exponent is odd.

problems.title: Multiply Strings
problems.summary: Multiply two non-negative integers given as strings.

approaches.name: Grade-school multiplication
steps: Initialize result array of size m+n.
Multiply each digit pair, add to result[i+j+1] with carry.
Propagate carries through result.
Strip leading zeros and return string.
approaches.analogy: Like multiplying on paper — each digit pair contributes to the result array with carry.

problems.title: Detect Squares
problems.summary: Count number of axis-aligned squares with given points as corners.

approaches.name: Hash map point counts
steps: Store point frequencies in hash map.
For each pair (p1, p2), compute potential square corners.
Check if both missing corners exist in map.
Sum valid square counts (avoid double count).
approaches.analogy: Like finding squares from two corners — given two points, check if the other two corners exist in the map.

problems.title: Single Number
problems.summary: Return the single element that appears once (all others appear twice).

approaches.name: XOR all elements
steps: Initialize result = 0.
XOR each number with result.
Pairs cancel to 0; singleton remains.
Return result.
approaches.analogy: Like canceling pairs in a pile — XOR makes duplicates vanish, leaving the lone number.

problems.title: Number of 1 Bits
problems.summary: Return the number of set bits (1s) in unsigned 32-bit n.

approaches.name: n & (n-1) loop
steps: While n > 0, increment count.
Clear lowest set bit: n = n & (n-1).
Each iteration removes one 1-bit.
Return count.
approaches.analogy: Like peeling one layer off an onion — each n & (n−1) removes the lowest set bit until nothing remains.

approaches.name: Built-in bit count
steps: Use language bit_count or popcount on n.
Return the count of set bits.
O(1) or O(bits) depending on implementation.
approaches.analogy: Like using a popcount instruction — let the hardware count set bits directly.

problems.title: Counting Bits
problems.summary: Return array where ans[i] is number of 1 bits in binary representation of i.

approaches.name: DP bits[i] = bits[i>>1] + i&1
steps: bits[0] = 0.
For i from 1 to n: bits[i] = bits[i>>1] + (i & 1).
Right shift drops last bit; add its value.
Return bits array.
approaches.analogy: Like inheriting your parent's bit count — half the number's bits plus the last bit.

problems.title: Reverse Bits
problems.summary: Reverse the bits of a 32-bit unsigned integer.

approaches.name: Bit-by-bit reverse
steps: Initialize result = 0.
For 32 iterations, shift result left.
Append n's least significant bit to result.
Right shift n each step.
approaches.analogy: Like reading a binary number backwards — shift result left, grab the last bit of n, repeat 32 times.

problems.title: Missing Number
problems.summary: Find the missing number from array containing n distinct numbers in [0, n].

approaches.name: XOR index and values
steps: XOR all indices 0..n and all nums values.
Duplicate pairs cancel; missing remains.
Return XOR result.
O(n) time, O(1) space.
approaches.analogy: Like canceling every number with its index — the one that survives is the missing value.

approaches.name: Sum formula
steps: Expected sum = n*(n+1)/2.
Subtract actual sum of nums.
Difference is missing number.
Return missing value.
approaches.analogy: Like expected attendance minus actual headcount — the difference is who didn't show up.

problems.title: Sum of Two Integers
problems.summary: Sum two integers a and b without using + or - operators.

approaches.name: XOR + carry loop
steps: While carry != 0: sum = a ^ b.
carry = (a & b) << 1.
Update a = sum, b = carry.
Return a when carry is 0.
approaches.analogy: Like adding in binary without '+' — XOR gives sum bits, AND-shift gives carry; repeat until carry is zero.

problems.title: Reverse Integer
problems.summary: Reverse digits of 32-bit signed integer x; return 0 on overflow.

approaches.name: Extract digits with overflow check
steps: While x != 0, pop digit with x % 10.
Check overflow before push: res > INT_MAX/10 or boundary case.
Push digit: res = res*10 + digit.
Return res.
approaches.analogy: Like reversing an odometer with guardrails — pop digits from the right, stop before 32-bit overflow.

python.solution:
class Solution:
    def reverse(self, x):
        INT_MAX = 2**31 - 1
        INT_MIN = -2**31
        res = 0
        sign = -1 if x < 0 else 1
        x = abs(x)
        while x:
            res = res * 10 + x % 10
            x //= 10
        res *= sign
        if res > INT_MAX or res < INT_MIN:
            return 0
        return res
